steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the overall structure of the text. Identify the two main movements: the text first describes how others regard Mr. Ely (remarkable, learned, discreet, agreeable), then describes his specific behaviors (avoids heated arguments, suggests rather than asserts, never lets anyone see him mock them or mock him).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘It implies that Mr. Ely's neighbors are more naive in their estimation of him than people in London are and then explains why his neighbors have been so easily misled.’: The text draws no comparison between Milby neighbors and London people regarding naivety, and there's no suggestion anyone has been misled. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘It presents the favorable opinion of Mr. Ely that other people hold and then describes the behaviors of Mr. Ely that enable him to maintain that favorable opinion.’: First half = laity and clergy regard him as remarkable, learned, discreet, agreeable. Second half = his behaviors (avoiding arguments, suggesting rather than asserting, never giving grounds to mock or be mocked) that sustain this reputation. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘It shows that Mr. Ely had originally been admired in Milby but is now less well-regarded and then details the events that caused their regard for him to subside.’: The text shows only consistent admiration — there is no decline in reputation, and no events causing a change. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘It highlights the disparity between Mr. Ely's public and private behavior and then conveys why he labors to obscure his true self from other people.’: The text never reveals a hidden private self that differs from his public conduct — it simply describes his behavior, not a gap between public and private. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
